planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall at 3–4 inches depth in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; Michigan lily is one of the tallest native lilies and prefers consistently moist, fertile soil along stream banks, moist meadows, and moist prairie edges. Thrives with dappled afternoon shade.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun along stream banks, moist meadows, and moist prairie edges; Michigan lily is a tall native lily of the Midwest and Great Plains, naturally growing in moist prairies, stream banks, and open woodland margins. Like Turk's cap lily (L. superbum), it produces nodding, orange-red, spotted flowers with strongly reflexed petals, but Michigan lily is more common in the central and western part of the range where Turk's cap is absent. The tall stems can reach 7 feet, bearing up to a dozen or more flowers per stem in mid-summer. The drooping flowers are adapted for pollination by large, strong bumblebees and the great spangled fritillary butterfly. A superb native for moist meadow gardens, rain gardens, and naturalistic plantings in zones 3–8.

planting.note: Plant rhizomatous bulbs in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; leopard lily spreads by rhizomatous bulbs to form large colonies over time. It prefers stream-bank conditions with consistent moisture and partial shade but is more adaptable than many native lilies.

directions: Plant rhizomatous bulbs in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; leopard lily is the most widely cultivated and vigorous of the western native lilies, native to stream banks, seeps, and moist openings in the Sierra Nevada and Coast Ranges of California and southern Oregon. The reflexed orange flowers with red-spotted centers on tall, wiry stems are produced in midsummer. More adaptable than many native lilies — it spreads by interconnected rhizomatous bulbs to form substantial clumps and is considerably more heat- and drought-tolerant than it appears, provided consistent soil moisture is maintained at root depth. Naturalizes well in gardens with moist but well-drained conditions. The subspecies and cultivar 'Giganteum' grows to 8 feet with over 30 flowers per stem. An important parent of the classic 'Bellingham Hybrid' garden lilies.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in moist to seasonally wet, acidic, sandy or peaty, low-nutrient soil in full sun; pine lily grows naturally in wet pine flatwoods and savannas of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain. It requires fire-maintained, open, acidic, wet conditions and is imperiled by fire suppression and habitat loss.

directions: Plant in moist to seasonally wet, acidic, sandy or peaty, low-nutrient soil in full sun in wet pine flatwoods or bog garden conditions; pine lily is among the most spectacularly beautiful native flowers of the Gulf Coastal Plain, producing single, large, upward-facing flowers with orange-red petals spotted with purple and with a distinctive long, tapered tube base — unlike the nodding, reflexed form of most other native lilies. The upright, face-up flower orientation makes it a standout for pollinators and human observers alike. Unfortunately pine lily is increasingly imperiled throughout its range due to fire suppression (it requires periodic fire to maintain open pine flatwood habitat), drainage, and development. It is the larval host for the rare tiger moth species and an important nectar source for swallowtail butterflies. A critical species for pine flatwood restoration in zone 9.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination or direct sow in spring in well-drained, dry, slightly acidic, sandy to rocky, lean soil in partial shade to full sun; slender goldenrod is a woodland-edge and open woodland species of the southeastern Coastal Plain, more shade-tolerant than most goldenrods.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry, lean, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun in woodland edges, open woodlands, and dry, sandy gardens; slender goldenrod is a southeastern native with a more upright, slender, unbranched stem than most goldenrods, producing a narrow, terminal, spike-like golden flower cluster in late summer. Unlike many goldenrod species that form large, spreading clumps in sunny meadows, slender goldenrod is more at home in open woodland settings and tolerates more shade than most of its relatives. It is relatively restrained in growth and does not spread aggressively, making it well-suited to the native shade garden where its late-summer yellow flowers provide important nectar for migrating monarchs and late-season native bees. Drought-tolerant once established. An underused goldenrod for naturalistic southeastern gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; pale corydalis germinates in cool soil and self-sows freely in rocky, lean, acidic, well-drained soil. It is a biennial to short-lived perennial that grows naturally in disturbed rocky soils, talus slopes, and open rocky woods across northeastern North America.

directions: Direct sow in fall in rocky, well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; pale corydalis is a delicate, finely cut-leaved, blue-green biennial of rocky, disturbed soils in northeastern North America, producing small, tubular, pink-tipped flowers with yellow ends from spring through summer. The flowers have the distinctive spurring and shape of the Corydalis/Fumitory family, with petals fused into two pairs, the outer pair spurred. The feathery blue-green foliage is attractive throughout the growing season. Self-sows freely in appropriate rocky, lean conditions to persist indefinitely. An excellent plant for dry, rocky garden walls, scree beds, and disturbed rocky soil in partial shade. The flowers attract long-tongued bees and hummingbirds. Often establishes spontaneously in rocky garden edges and wall crevices in the Northeast.
